questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: In a galvanic (voltaic) cell, which electrode undergoes oxidation?

choices.explanation: The cathode is where reduction occurs (RED CAT); the anode is where oxidation occurs (AN OX).
In a galvanic cell, the anode is the negative electrode where the reducing agent is oxidized, releasing electrons that flow through the external circuit to the cathode.
The salt bridge maintains electrical neutrality by allowing ion flow; it does not perform oxidation or reduction.
In a galvanic cell, one electrode undergoes oxidation (anode) and the other undergoes reduction (cathode) — never both simultaneously.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Calculate $E^\circ_{\text{cell}}$ for the reaction $\text{Zn(s)} + \text{Cu}^{2+}\text{(aq)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + \text{Cu(s)}$. ($E^\circ_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.34$ V; $E^\circ_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}} = -0.76$ V)

choices.explanation: $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}} = +0.34 - (-0.76) = +1.10$ V; not −0.42 V.
Cu²⁺ is reduced (cathode, higher $E^\circ$); Zn is oxidized (anode, lower $E^\circ$): $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}} = +0.34 - (-0.76) = +1.10$ V.
A negative cell potential would indicate a non-spontaneous reaction. Cu²⁺/Zn gives a spontaneous (positive) cell potential of +1.10 V.
$0.42$ V would result from incorrectly subtracting: $0.76 - 0.34 = 0.42$; but the correct formula uses $E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}} = 0.34 - (-0.76) = 1.10$ V.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: What is $\Delta G^\circ$ for a reaction where $n = 2$ electrons are transferred and $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = +0.550$ V? ($F = 96{,}485$ C/mol)

choices.explanation: $\Delta G^\circ$ is negative for a spontaneous reaction ($E^\circ > 0$); the correct answer is $-106$ kJ/mol.
$-53.1$ kJ would result from $n = 1$; the problem specifies $n = 2$: $\Delta G^\circ = -2 \times 96485 \times 0.550 = -106$ kJ/mol.
$-212$ kJ would result from $n = 4$ or doubling the calculation; the correct result is $-106$ kJ/mol for $n = 2$.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Using the Nernst equation, what happens to the cell potential $E$ when reactant concentrations are increased above standard conditions ($1$ M)?

choices.explanation: Increasing reactant concentrations makes $Q < 1$; $\ln Q < 0$; $E = E^\circ - (RT/nF)\ln Q > E^\circ$; $E$ increases.
$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$; increasing reactant concentrations decreases $Q$ (below 1); $\ln Q < 0$; subtracting a negative number increases $E$ above $E^\circ$.
$E = E^\circ$ only at standard conditions (all concentrations 1 M); any deviation from standard concentrations changes $E$ via the Nernst equation.
$E = 0$ occurs only at equilibrium; increasing reactant concentrations moves the system further from equilibrium, not toward it.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: For a galvanic cell with $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = +0.463$ V and $n = 2$, what is the equilibrium constant $K$ at 298 K? ($R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K))

choices.explanation: $K = E^\circ$ has no mathematical basis; $K$ is calculated from $\Delta G^\circ = -RT\ln K = -nFE^\circ$.
36.0 is $\ln K$, not $K$ itself; $K = e^{36.0} \approx 4.3 \times 10^{15}$.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: In the electrolysis of water, at which electrode does $\text{O}_2\text{(g)}$ form?

choices.explanation: At the cathode: $2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + 2\text{OH}^-$; hydrogen gas forms, not oxygen.
At the anode: $2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^-$; water is oxidized to produce oxygen gas at the anode.
Oxygen forms only at the anode (oxidation); hydrogen forms at the cathode (reduction) in the electrolysis of water.
The salt bridge facilitates ion flow; it is not an electrode and no oxidation or reduction occurs there.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: How many grams of copper can be deposited on an electrode if a current of 2.00 A flows for 30.0 minutes during the electrolysis of $\text{CuSO}_4\text{(aq)}$? (Molar mass of Cu = 63.55 g/mol; $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol; Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu)

choices.explanation: 2.37 g would result from using $n = 1$ instead of $n = 2$ (Cu²⁺ requires 2 electrons per atom); mass = $0.01865/1 \times 63.55$... actually $n=1$ gives 2.37 g — this is incorrect because Cu²⁺ is reduced with 2e⁻.
4.74 g would require twice the correct amount of charge or half the number of electrons per Cu atom.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following correctly represents the three-way relationship in electrochemistry?

choices.explanation: These signs are inconsistent: $\Delta G^\circ > 0$ means non-spontaneous, but $E^\circ > 0$ and $K > 1$ mean spontaneous. They cannot all be true simultaneously.

$\Delta G^\circ < 0$ → spontaneous; $E^\circ > 0$ → spontaneous galvanic cell; $K > 1$ → products favored. All three consistently describe a spontaneous process, linked by $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ = -RT\ln K$.
$\Delta G^\circ < 0$ and $K > 1$ are consistent with spontaneous, but $E^\circ < 0$ is not; the three must all point in the same direction.
All three signs indicate a non-spontaneous reaction (in the forward direction), not spontaneous.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: What is the purpose of the salt bridge in a galvanic cell?

choices.text: To allow electrons to flow between the two half-cells
choices.explanation: Electrons flow through the external wire (circuit), not through the salt bridge.

choices.text: To maintain electrical neutrality by allowing ions to flow between half-cells
choices.explanation: As the cell operates, charge builds up in each half-cell (excess positive or negative ions); the salt bridge allows anions and cations to migrate to maintain charge neutrality in both compartments.
The salt bridge maintains electrical neutrality, which allows the cell to continue operating; it doesn't directly increase $E^\circ_{\text{cell}}$.

choices.text: To act as a catalyst for the electrochemical reaction
choices.explanation: A salt bridge does not lower activation energy or catalyze reactions; it only provides a pathway for ion migration to maintain charge balance.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Using the Nernst equation at 25°C, what is the cell potential $E$ for the Zn/Cu galvanic cell when $[\text{Zn}^{2+}] = 0.100$ M and $[\text{Cu}^{2+}] = 0.0100$ M? ($E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = +1.10$ V, $n = 2$)

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: For the Zn/Cu cell ($E^\circ = +1.10$ V, $n = 2$) at 25°C with $[\text{Zn}^{2+}] = 0.100$ M and $[\text{Cu}^{2+}] = 0.0100$ M, which statement is correct?

choices.text: $E < E^\circ$ because $Q > 1$ (more products than reactants relative to standard)
choices.explanation: $Q = [\text{Zn}^{2+}]/[\text{Cu}^{2+}] = 10 > 1$; $E = E^\circ - (0.0592/2)\log(10) = 1.10 - 0.0296 = 1.07$ V $< 1.10$ V = $E^\circ$. Higher product/reactant ratio reduces cell potential.
$E = E^\circ$ only at standard concentrations (1 M each); non-standard concentrations change $E$ via the Nernst equation.

choices.text: $E > E^\circ$ because the reaction has proceeded
choices.explanation: $Q = 10 > 1$ means the reaction is leaning toward products; applying Nernst equation gives $E < E^\circ$.

choices.text: $E = 0$ V because the system is at equilibrium
choices.explanation: $E = 0$ requires $Q = K$; here $Q = 10$ while $K \approx 10^{37}$ for this cell; the system is far from equilibrium.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: In an electrolytic cell used for electroplating gold ($\text{Au}^{3+} + 3e^- \rightarrow \text{Au}$), where does gold plate out?

choices.text: At the anode (positive electrode in electrolytic cell)
choices.explanation: In an electrolytic cell, the anode is positive and undergoes oxidation; gold plating requires reduction ($\text{Au}^{3+} \rightarrow \text{Au}$), which occurs at the cathode.

choices.text: At the cathode (negative electrode in electrolytic cell)
choices.explanation: In an electrolytic cell, the cathode is the negative electrode where reduction occurs; $\text{Au}^{3+}$ ions are attracted to the negative cathode and are reduced to $\text{Au}^0$ metal.
The salt bridge allows ion migration but is not an electrode; metal deposition occurs only at electrode surfaces.
Electroplating deposits metal specifically on the electrode surface (cathode), not uniformly throughout the solution.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which statement correctly describes the standard hydrogen electrode (SHE)?

choices.text: It has $E^\circ = +1.00$ V and is used as the reference for all reduction potentials
choices.explanation: The SHE is defined as $E^\circ = 0.000$ V by convention, not +1.00 V.

choices.text: It has $E^\circ = 0.000$ V and consists of $\text{H}_2$ gas at 1 atm over a platinum electrode in 1 M $\text{H}^+$ solution
choices.explanation: The SHE consists of $\text{H}_2\text{(g)}$ at 1 atm bubbling over a platinized Pt electrode in 1.00 M $\text{H}^+\text{(aq)}$; by convention $E^\circ = 0.000$ V, and all other electrode potentials are measured relative to it.

choices.text: It measures the potential of hydrogen bonds between molecules
choices.explanation: The SHE measures the electrochemical potential of the $\text{H}^+/\text{H}_2$ half-reaction, not hydrogen bonding between molecules.

choices.text: It can only be used as an anode, not a cathode
choices.explanation: The SHE can be either anode or cathode depending on the half-reaction compared to it; it is a reference electrode, not restricted to one function.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which metal would best protect an iron pipeline from corrosion when connected to it as a sacrificial anode? ($E^\circ$: Fe²⁺/Fe = −0.44 V; Mg²⁺/Mg = −2.37 V; Cu²⁺/Cu = +0.34 V; Zn²⁺/Zn = −0.76 V)

choices.explanation: Copper has a higher reduction potential than iron ($+0.34 > -0.44$ V); copper would be reduced while iron would be oxidized — the opposite of cathodic protection.
Zinc ($E^\circ = -0.76$ V) is more active than iron and would work as a sacrificial anode, but magnesium ($E^\circ = -2.37$ V) is even more active and more effective.

For cathodic protection, the sacrificial anode must be more easily oxidized (lower $E^\circ$) than iron. Magnesium ($E^\circ = -2.37$ V) has the lowest (most negative) reduction potential; it is most readily oxidized, making it the best sacrificial anode.
Using the same metal provides no electrochemical driving force; a more active metal must be used as the sacrificial anode.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: For the spontaneous cell reaction $\text{Cu(s)} + 2\text{Ag}^+\text{(aq)} \rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2\text{Ag(s)}$, $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = +0.46$ V. What is $\Delta G^\circ$? ($n = 2$; $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol)

choices.explanation: A positive $E^\circ$ gives negative $\Delta G^\circ$ (spontaneous); $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ = -(2)(96485)(0.46) = -88{,}767$ J $\approx -88.8$ kJ/mol.
$\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ = -(2)(96{,}485)(0.46) = -88{,}767$ J/mol $\approx -88.8$ kJ/mol. Negative $\Delta G^\circ$ confirms spontaneity.
$-177.6$ kJ would result from $n = 4$; the problem specifies $n = 2$ (2 Ag⁺ each gaining 1e⁻).
0.46 is $E^\circ$ in volts; $\Delta G^\circ$ requires multiplication by $nF$: $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ = -88.8$ kJ/mol.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a condition of the standard state used in electrochemistry?

choices.explanation: Standard conditions require 1 M ionic concentrations; this IS a standard condition.
Standard conditions are defined at 298 K; this IS a standard condition.
Standard conditions require 1 atm gas pressure; this IS a standard condition.

While ideality is sometimes assumed in introductory electrochemistry, the AP Chemistry standard conditions refer to temperature (298 K), concentration (1 M), and pressure (1 atm); activity coefficient specification goes beyond standard AP Chemistry scope and is NOT typically listed as a standard condition.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: For the reaction with $\Delta G^\circ = +20$ kJ/mol, which statement about $K$ and $E^\circ_{\text{cell}}$ (with $n = 2$) is correct?

choices.explanation: $\Delta G^\circ > 0$ means non-spontaneous, so $K < 1$ and $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} < 0$.
$\Delta G^\circ = +20$ kJ/mol > 0 → non-spontaneous; $K = e^{-\Delta G^\circ/(RT)} = e^{-8.07} < 1$; $E^\circ = -\Delta G^\circ/(nF) = -20000/(2 \times 96485) = -0.104$ V < 0.
$K = 1$ and $E^\circ = 0$ correspond to $\Delta G^\circ = 0$; here $\Delta G^\circ = +20$ kJ/mol $\neq 0$.
$K > 1$ requires $\Delta G^\circ < 0$ (products favored); here $\Delta G^\circ > 0$ so $K < 1$. The signs of $K$ and $E^\circ$ must be consistent with $\Delta G^\circ$.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: At equilibrium, the cell potential $E$ for a galvanic cell equals:

choices.explanation: $E = E^\circ$ only at standard conditions ($Q = 1$); at equilibrium, $Q = K$ and $E = 0$.
At equilibrium, $Q = K$ and $\Delta G = 0$; since $\Delta G = -nFE$, if $\Delta G = 0$ then $E = 0$. The galvanic cell can no longer do work; the battery is 'dead'.
$-E^\circ$ would apply if the reaction ran in reverse under standard conditions; at equilibrium, $E = 0$, not $-E^\circ$.
As a galvanic cell discharges toward equilibrium, $E$ decreases from $E^\circ$ toward 0; $E$ cannot exceed $E^\circ$ during spontaneous discharge.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following describes an electrolytic cell?

choices.text: A cell that converts spontaneous chemical energy to electrical energy
choices.explanation: This describes a galvanic (voltaic) cell, not an electrolytic cell.

choices.text: A cell that uses external electrical energy to drive a non-spontaneous reaction
choices.explanation: Electrolytic cells require electrical energy input (from an external source like a battery) to drive a non-spontaneous redox reaction; examples include electroplating and water electrolysis.

choices.text: A cell where the anode is the positive electrode and reduction occurs there
choices.explanation: In electrolytic cells, the anode IS positive — but the anode is where OXIDATION occurs (not reduction). Reduction occurs at the cathode (negative electrode).
Galvanic cells have $E^\circ > 0$; electrolytic cells have $E^\circ < 0$ (non-spontaneous reaction driven by external power).

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: For the Nernst equation $E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$, what is $Q$ for the reaction $\text{Zn(s)} + \text{Cu}^{2+}\text{(aq)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + \text{Cu(s)}$?

choices.explanation: The reaction quotient puts products in the numerator and reactants in the denominator; pure solids are excluded.
Pure solids (Zn and Cu) have activity = 1 and are excluded from $Q$; only aqueous ion concentrations appear: $Q = [\text{Zn}^{2+}]/[\text{Cu}^{2+}]$.
Products go in the numerator; $\text{Zn}^{2+}$ is a product (formed from Zn solid being oxidized) and belongs in the numerator.
Pure solids Zn and Cu are excluded from $Q$ expressions; only aqueous concentrations appear.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Corrosion of iron involves which of the following half-reactions at the anodic site?

choices.explanation: This is the reduction half-reaction; at the anodic site, oxidation occurs (iron loses electrons to form Fe²⁺).
At the anode of the corrosion cell, iron is oxidized: $\text{Fe(s)} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + 2e^-$. The released electrons travel to the cathodic site where $\text{O}_2$ is reduced.
This is the cathodic (reduction) half-reaction in corrosion; oxygen is reduced at the cathodic site, not the anodic site.
This is another possible cathodic reaction; the anodic (oxidation) reaction for iron corrosion is Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻.
